title: The Mortal Who Refused to Be Expelled
content:
The Celestial Sword Sect's dismissal hall smelled of sandalwood and failure.

Ren Wei stood in the center of the polished jade floor, his forehead pressed against the cold stone in the deepest kowtow the sect allowed. The gesture was ceremonial now—he'd done it seventeen times in the last hour. Each time, the Elders on their elevated platform had watched with the same expression: mild inconvenience, like swatting a persistent fly.

"Disciple Ren Wei," Elder Huo announced, his voice carrying the weight of three centuries of cultivation, "your meridians have been assessed by the Grand Examination Array. The results are final."

Ren didn't look up. He'd memorized the grain pattern in the jade tiles. It looked like a river. A river that would never flow through his spiritual channels.

"Your cultivation base: Qi Gathering, third stage. Your age: nineteen. Your potential: limited."

*Limited.* Such a polite word for *worthless*.

"The Celestial Sword Sect cannot invest further resources in a disciple who will never reach Foundation Establishment. By the authority vested in this council, you are hereby—"

"I'll pay double."

The words escaped before Ren could stop them. He raised his head, meeting Elder Huo's startled gaze. The old man's beard twitched.

"Excuse me?"

"Double the contribution points. Triple, even." Ren's voice cracked, but he pressed on. "I know I'm not talented, but I work hard. I've memorized every manual in the Outer Library. I can identify seventeen types of spirit herbs by smell alone. I—"

"You can't cultivate," Elder Huo interrupted, his tone softening to something worse than anger: pity. "The Array has spoken. Your meridians are like cracked pots. No amount of qi will fill them. This is not a punishment, Disciple Ren. It is reality."

*Reality.* As if reality couldn't be changed. As if the world wasn't built by people stubborn enough to refuse their limitations.

Ren rose slowly, brushing the dust from his gray outer robes. The dismissal hall stretched around him, vast enough to hold five hundred disciples. Today, it held only him, seven Elders, and his shattered future.

"I understand," he said, his voice flat.

He didn't understand at all.

The sect gate closed behind him with a sound like a tombstone sealing.

Ren stood on the mountain path, his belongings tied in a cloth bundle: three changes of robes, a chipped jade pendant that held no spiritual power, and a scroll on basic formations he'd borrowed from the library six months ago. Technically, it was theft now. He didn't care.

The Celestial Sword Sect rose behind him, its pagodas piercing clouds that glowed with the ambient qi of a thousand cultivators meditating in harmony. Below, the mortal world stretched in muddy roads and small villages. A world where he'd be just another peasant with calloused hands and empty dantian.

"That's it?" he muttered to himself. "Nineteen years of training, and I'm back to farming turnips?"

The wind carried the scent of wild ginger from the forest below. Somewhere, a bird sang a mocking melody.

Ren's fist tightened around his bundle. His spiritual sense was weak, barely enough to sense qi at all, but he could still feel the sect's protective formation humming behind him. A formation designed to keep threats out—and failures like him in their proper place.

*Fine.*

He turned his back on the Celestial Sword Sect.

*If the mountain won't have me, I'll build my own mountain.*

---

Three days later, Ren realized that building a mountain was significantly harder than the inspirational metaphor suggested.

He'd walked forty-three miles south, following a road that grew progressively less maintained until it became a deer trail, then a suggestion, then nothing at all. His feet bled through his straw sandals. His last rice cake had dissolved into crumbs in his pocket. And the only thing he'd found was a temple so decrepit it looked like it was actively trying to collapse.

"Perfect," he said, his voice hoarse with thirst.

The temple sat in a clearing that had once been a courtyard, now overgrown with weeds that reached his waist. The main hall's roof had caved in on one side, exposing faded murals of celestial dragons to the elements. The wooden pillars were pitted with rot and insect holes. A stone plaque above the entrance bore characters so worn he could barely read them: *Temple of the—* something. The third character was completely gone.

Ren pushed open the door. It fell off its hinges.

Inside, the temple was worse. Rain had pooled on the dirt floor, creating a small ecosystem of moss and mushrooms. A broken statue of some forgotten immortal lay in pieces near the altar, its serene face cracked in half. The air smelled of wet stone, dead leaves, and despair.

*Perfect,* Ren thought again, and this time he almost believed it.

The Celestial Sword Sect had pristine training grounds, libraries filled with ancient texts, and meditation chambers lined with spirit stones. They had everything a cultivator could want.

This temple had nothing.

Which meant Ren had nothing to lose.

He dropped his bundle in the driest corner, which was still damp enough to soak through his robes in an hour. Then he sat cross-legged on the floor, closed his eyes, and attempted to cultivate.

The qi in this area was thin. Pathetic, really. Like trying to drink soup through a clogged straw. Ren's damaged meridians struggled to draw even a wisp of spiritual energy, and what little he captured leaked out almost immediately.

*Cracked pots,* Elder Huo had said.

Ren opened his eyes. "Right. First problem: location. This place has terrible qi density."

He stood, ignoring the protest from his blistered feet, and walked outside. The temple grounds stretched about two acres, bordered by a crumbling wall on three sides and a cliff on the fourth. Beyond the cliff, a valley spread out like a green carpet, dotted with the roofs of farming villages.

No spirit veins. No natural formations. No spiritual herbs growing wild.

"Second problem: no resources." Ren counted on his fingers. "Third problem: no disciples. Fourth problem: no reputation. Fifth problem: no money."

He paused.

"Actually, that's everything. I have nothing."

For a moment, the weight of his situation pressed down on him like a mountain. He was a failed cultivator in an abandoned temple in the middle of nowhere, with nothing but the clothes on his back and a stolen formations scroll.

Then he laughed.

It wasn't a sane laugh. It was the laugh of a man who had hit rock bottom and discovered that rock bottom came with an unexpected benefit: you couldn't fall any further.

"Fine," Ren said to the empty temple, to the uncaring sky, to the entire cultivation world that had written him off. "If I have nothing, then I'll build from nothing. That's a better story anyway."

He pulled out his formations scroll and unrolled it on a relatively clean patch of ground. The paper was yellowed, the characters written in careful brushstrokes that spoke of a master's hand.

*Basic Qi Gathering Formation,* the title read. *For use in areas of low spiritual density. Requires: formation ink, spirit stones (4), brush of medium quality, stable foundation.*

Ren looked at the list. He had none of those things.

An hour later, he'd gathered materials that were *almost* what he needed. Charcoal from the temple's old cooking fire served as ink. A sharpened stick replaced the brush. He'd found four smooth river stones that had no spiritual power whatsoever but looked nice. And the temple floor was dirt, which was technically a foundation, if not a stable one.

"Improvisation," he said, drawing the formation's outer circle with charcoal-stick precision. "That's what startups do."

The formation took three hours to complete. His back ached, his fingers were black with soot, and the design looked nothing like the elegant diagrams in the scroll. But when he placed the final river stone at the formation's center and pushed a thread of qi into the activation node—

*Something happened.*

The air stirred. Dust motes danced in the dim light filtering through the collapsed roof. And for just a moment, Ren felt the qi in the temple *move*, drawn toward his crude formation like water seeking the lowest point.

*It works,* he realized, his heart pounding. *My garbage, pathetic, barely-functional formation actually works.*

The feeling lasted three seconds before the river stone cracked, the charcoal lines smeared, and the formation collapsed in a puff of impotent energy.

But for those three seconds, Ren had felt it: possibility.

He was still grinning when the drunk stumbled through the doorway.

---

The man was a disaster in human form.

His robes were the color of old tea stains, patched in a dozen places with mismatched cloth. His hair hung in greasy tangles around a face that might have been handsome once, before a lifetime of alcohol had blurred its edges. A gourd of wine hung from his belt, and he clutched a sword so rusted it looked like it might crumble if he swung it too hard.

He took one look at Ren, one look at the failed formation, and said: "That's the worst formation I've ever seen."

"Thank you," Ren replied, because he wasn't sure how else to respond.

The drunk stumbled further into the temple, his movements uncoordinated in a way that seemed almost theatrical. He tripped over a loose stone, spun in an elaborate circle that somehow kept him upright, and collapsed onto a pile of debris near the altar.

"Nice place," he said, taking a long drink from his gourd. "Very... abandoned. Is it yours?"

"Just got it."

"Ah. New management." The drunk belched. "I'm Li Feng. Used to be the Sword Saint of the Azure Peaks. Now I'm just... Li Feng." He held out the gourd. "Want some?"

Ren stared at the offered wine. His mother had taught him never to accept drinks from strangers, especially strangers who claimed to be legendary sword saints while looking like they'd slept in a ditch.

"I'm Ren Wei," he said, ignoring the gourd. "Founder of the New Dao Sect."

Li Feng's bloodshot eyes widened. "New Dao Sect? Never heard of it."

"That's because I founded it approximately four hours ago."

For a long moment, the drunk just stared at him. Then he started laughing—a wheezing, hacking sound that turned into a coughing fit that nearly knocked him off his debris throne.

"You," Li Feng gasped, pointing a shaking finger at Ren, "are either the bravest man I've ever met or the stupidest."

"Both," Ren admitted. "I'm told it's a common trait among founders."

Li Feng's laughter died down into chuckles. He took another drink, studying Ren with eyes that suddenly seemed sharper than they had any right to be. "A sect, huh? What's your cultivation level?"

"Qi Gathering, third stage."

"Ha! That's barely above mortal."

"I know."

"And you want to start a sect? Compete with the Celestial Sword Sect, the Crimson Phoenix Palace, the—"

"I don't want to compete," Ren interrupted. "I want to build something better."

Li Feng tilted his head. "Better how?"

Ren thought about it. The Celestial Sword Sect had rejected him for his lack of talent. The cultivation world was built on hierarchies of natural ability, where those born with wide meridians and strong spiritual roots rose while those like him were cast aside.

"What if," Ren said slowly, "a sect didn't care about talent? What if it accepted anyone—the failures, the misfits, the people the old sects threw away—and taught them to succeed

title: The Business Plan in Blood
content:
The morning sun did nothing to warm the crumbling temple.

Ren stood in what had once been the main hall, staring at a roof that let in more light than it kept out. Three pillars remained standing. The fourth had collapsed sometime during the night, taking a chunk of the eastern wall with it. Dust motes danced in the golden shafts of sunlight, and somewhere in the rafters, something that sounded suspiciously like a nest of spirit rats squeaked in protest.

"Perfect," Ren muttered, pulling out a roll of parchment. "Absolutely perfect."

He'd spent the entire night awake, fueled by indignation and the last of his qi reserves. The Celestial Sword Sect had thrown him out like spoiled meat. Fine. Let them. While they were busy polishing their precious swords and bowing to elders who hadn't had an original thought in three centuries, Ren would build something that would make them choke on their own superiority.

The problem was that his new sect currently consisted of:

One collapsing temple.
Zero disciples.
A business plan written in blood. 
(His own blood. That part was important.)

He unrolled the parchment, revealing a mess of characters that looked like they'd been written by a drunk spider. The ink had smeared in places, and there was definitely a tea stain near the section labeled "REVENUE STREAMS."

"Step one: Recruitment," Ren read aloud, his voice echoing in the empty hall. "Step two: Secure funding. Step three: Establish sect reputation. Step four: Profit."

He stared at the plan.

It was terrible.

No, that was generous. It was catastrophic. He had no money, no connections, and the only thing his former sect had given him was a severance package consisting of exactly three low-grade spirit stones and a scroll that said "Better luck next life."

But here was the thing about being at rock bottom—there was nowhere to go but up.

"New Dao Sect," Ren said, testing the name on his tongue. It sounded ridiculous. A startup with no product, no team, and no capital. The celestial business world would laugh at him.

Let them laugh.

He was about to start sketching out a more detailed plan when the temple's front door—which was really just a wooden slab leaning against the entrance—crashed open.

Ren's hand went to his sword.

A figure stumbled through, coughing and waving away dust. It was a woman, maybe thirty years old, wearing robes that had once been fine silk but were now stained with what looked like wine, mud, and something that might have been blood. She carried a gourd on her hip and a sword on her back, and she was grinning like she'd just won a bet.

"Nice place," she said, her voice rough. "Very... rustic."

Ren didn't lower his hand. "Who are you?"

"Name's Li Mei." She bowed, slightly unsteady on her feet. "Former Sword Saint of the Azure Cloud Sect. Well, 'former' is doing a lot of work there. I was more of a Sword Apprentice, really. But Saint sounds better, don't you think?"

"You're drunk."

"Perceptive." Li Mei pulled the gourd from her hip and took a long swig. "Want some? It's not good wine, but it's wine."

"No."

"Suit yourself." She wiped her mouth with her sleeve and looked around the temple with an expression that was equal parts amusement and pity. "I heard there was a new sect starting up in these parts. Thought I'd check it out. See if there were any opportunities."

Ren's eyes narrowed. "How did you hear about it?"

"Word travels fast when a Celestial Sword Sect reject starts shouting about building his own empire." She shrugged. "Also, I was in the tavern next door when you got kicked out. Good show, by the way. Very dramatic."

Great. His reputation was already spreading.

"Why are you here?" Ren asked, keeping his voice level. "If you're looking for a cultivator sect, there are a dozen better ones within a day's travel."

"Better ones, sure." Li Mei took another drink. "But they all have rules. Discipline. Expectations." She made a face. "I don't do well with expectations."

Ren studied her. A drunk sword master who'd been kicked out of her own sect. On paper, she was exactly the kind of person he should avoid. Unreliable. Probably unstable. Definitely a liability.

But she was also a sword cultivator. And she was standing in his temple.

"What's your cultivation level?" he asked.

"Middle stage of the Core Formation realm. Was peak, but I had a bad year." She didn't elaborate, and Ren didn't ask.

It was still impressive. Core Formation was nothing to sneeze at. The Celestial Sword Sect's junior disciples were mostly at the Qi Condensation level. Having someone with real power, even diminished, could give his sect some legitimacy.

"What do you want?" Ren asked.

"A place to sleep. Some wine occasionally. And—" She paused, her grin fading slightly. "—a chance to prove that I'm not just a washed-up drunk."

There it was. The desperation hiding behind the bravado.

Ren unrolled his parchment again. "I can't pay you."

"I figured."

"I can't offer you resources or training."

"Also figured."

"I can offer you a share in the sect's future profits, a voice in major decisions, and the satisfaction of watching the old powers choke on their own arrogance."

Li Mei's eyebrows rose. "That's... surprisingly ambitious for someone whose temple is falling apart."

"Ambitious is the only thing I have left."

She was quiet for a long moment. Then she laughed—a genuine laugh, not the hollow one she'd been using before. "Fine. I'm in. What's the plan, sect leader?"

Ren felt something lighten in his chest. He had a disciple. One disciple. A drunk, disgraced sword cultivator who might stab him in the back for a bottle of decent rice wine.

But it was a start.

"Step one," Ren said, pointing to his parchment. "We need more people. I have a list of potential recruits—outcasts, rejects, people who've been failed by the system."

"Sounds like a collection of misfits."

"Exactly." Ren smiled, and it wasn't a pleasant smile. "The old sects only take the best. The talented. The connected. We'll take the ones they threw away and turn them into something they never expected."

Li Mei whistled. "That's either brilliant or suicidal."

"Probably both."

"Where do we start?"

Ren looked at his list. The first name was circled in red ink. "A formation expert. Name's Jiang Feng. Heard he got banned from the Heavenly Array Sect for gambling debts. Rumor has it he's been living in the Ghost Bamboo Forest, avoiding debt collectors."

"Ghost Bamboo Forest?" Li Mei shuddered. "That place is cursed."

"Perfect. He'll fit right in."

---

The Ghost Bamboo Forest lived up to its name.

The bamboo stalks were bone-white, swaying even when there was no wind. A faint mist clung to the ground, and every so often, Ren could swear he saw shapes moving in the corner of his vision—shadows that vanished when he turned to look.

"This is bad luck," Li Mei muttered, her hand on her sword. "Very bad luck. I can feel the resentful qi in the air."

"Keep moving."

They'd been walking for two hours, following a trail of discarded spirit stone fragments and empty wine bottles. Whoever Jiang Feng was, he had a drinking problem that rivaled Li Mei's.

Finally, they found him.

The formation expert was sitting in a small clearing, surrounded by what looked like the remains of a dozen failed arrays. Glowing lines of energy flickered and died around him as he hunched over a piece of parchment, muttering to himself.

"Three directional fluxes," he said, scratching his head. "No, that's wrong. Four. Or maybe it's five. Why does the universe hate me?"

He looked up as Ren and Li Mei approached. He was a thin man, maybe forty, with hollow cheeks and eyes that darted nervously. His robes were patched in a dozen places, and his fingers were stained with ink and something that smelled faintly of sulfur.

"Who are you?" Jiang Feng demanded. "If you're here to collect debts, I don't have any money. If you're here to kill me, please get in line."

"Neither," Ren said. "I'm here to offer you a job."

Jiang Feng blinked. "A job?"

"I'm starting a sect. I need a formation expert."

"You need—" Jiang Feng laughed, a hysterical edge to it. "Do you know who I am? I'm banned from every respectable sect in three provinces. I owe more spirit stones than I can count. The Heavenly Array Sect put a bounty on my head—fifty spirit stones, alive or dead. Fifty! I'm worth less than a decent sword!"

"Seventy," Ren said.

"What?"

"I'll pay you seventy spirit stones per month. Plus a share of sect profits."

Jiang Feng's mouth opened and closed. "But... but I'm a failure. I'm washed up. I'm—"

"You're a formation expert who reached the Array Master level before you were thirty," Ren interrupted. "I read your file. You designed an array that could power an entire city district. The Heavenly Array Sect didn't ban you because you were bad. They banned you because you were better than them, and they couldn't control you."

The words hung in the air.

Jiang Feng's expression shifted from confusion to something raw and vulnerable. "You... you know about that?"

"I know everything."

It was a lie. Ren had read exactly one report, and half of it was illegible. But it was a useful lie.

"Why would you want me?" Jiang Feng asked. "I'm a liability. I'm—"

"I'm a Celestial Sword Sect reject. My sword master here is a drunk who got kicked out of the Azure Cloud Sect." Ren gestured at Li Mei, who waved cheerfully. "We're all liabilities. That's the point. The old sects only want perfect pieces. We're building something from the broken ones."

Jiang Feng stared at him for a long moment. Then, slowly, a smile spread across his face.

"Seventy spirit stones?"

"Seventy."

"And I get to design whatever arrays I want?"

"Within reason."

"Deal." Jiang Feng scrambled to his feet and grabbed a bag that was absolutely overflowing with formation discs. "Where do I sign?"

---

They found the chef by accident.

They were walking back to the temple, discussing plans for recruitment, when they smelled it. Roasted meat. Spices. Something that made Ren's stomach growl so loudly that Li Mei burst out laughing.

"Someone's cooking," she said. "And they're good at it."

The source was a small camp near the road. A man in his fifties, with a round face and flour-dusted robes, was stirring a pot over a fire. He had the look of someone who'd been cooking for decades—efficient, practiced, completely at peace.

"Hello there!" the man called out, spotting them. "Hungry? I've got plenty."

Ren hesitated. Taking food from a stranger in the cultivation world was a good way to get poisoned.

But the smell was irresistible.

"Who are you?" Ren asked.

"Name's Chen Wei. Former head chef of the Golden Lotus Sect's kitchens." He smiled, but there was a sadness in his eyes. "Well, 'former' is the key word. They kicked me out last week."

"Why?"

Chen Wei's smile faltered. "I accidentally infused my cooking with qi. The elders said it

title: The Investor Who Wanted Everything
content:
The morning sun painted the crumbling temple walls in shades of gold and betrayal.

Ren stood in what he now called the "main hall" — a polite term for a room with half a roof and a suspicious number of bird nests — and stared at the letter in his hands. The paper was crisp, expensive, the kind that minor sect leaders used to pretend they were major sect leaders. The ink smelled of jasmine and desperation.

"Who delivered this?" he asked.

Li Chen, his first disciple and the only one who'd shown up before noon, pointed a thumb toward the entrance. "Some kid in green robes. Ran off before I could ask questions. Said it was from the 'Azure Pond Trading Consortium.'"

Ren's blood ran cold.

The Azure Pond. One of the three great trading consortiums of the cultivation world. They didn't just move spirit stones — they moved entire economies. If a sect wanted pills, weapons, or political influence, they went through Azure Pond. If Azure Pond wanted you gone, you disappeared so thoroughly that even your ancestors forgot you existed.

And they were writing to him.

He broke the seal and read.

*Ren Wei, Founder of New Dao Sect,*

*We have reviewed your preliminary business proposal with great interest. The concept of a "startup sect" operating outside traditional cultivation hierarchies presents... unique opportunities.*

*We would like to invite you to a preliminary investment meeting at the Azure Pavilion, Jade District, three days hence. Bring your financials, your projections, and your most persuasive arguments.*

*Success may change everything for you.*

*Failure will not be forgotten.*

*— Consortium Master Huo, Strategic Investments Division*

Ren read the letter three times. Then a fourth, just to make sure the words hadn't rearranged themselves into a threat.

"Boss?" Li Chen leaned over his shoulder, squinting. "Is that good or bad?"

"Both," Ren said slowly. "It's the kind of meeting that either launches a sect or buries it."

"So... good?"

"Li Chen, do you know what 'failure will not be forgotten' means in consortium-speak?"

"No."

"It means if I waste their time, they'll make sure I never cultivate again. They'll blacklist me from every pill seller, every weapon forger, every formation master within a thousand li. I'll be a mortal with a qi deficiency and a very impressive collection of debt."

Li Chen nodded thoughtfully. "So we should probably prepare."

"Prepare?" Ren laughed, the sound hollow in the empty hall. "Li Chen, we have three disciples, a roof that leaks, and exactly seventeen low-grade spirit stones to our name. Our 'financials' are written on a napkin stained with cooking oil. Our 'projections' are optimistic guesses based on the fact that I once read a book about economics."

"Seventeen spirit stones?" Li Chen's eyes widened. "That's... that's almost enough for a decent sword."

"It's almost enough for a decent meal at a roadside inn. It is not enough to impress a consortium that moves more wealth in a day than most sects see in a century."

Ren crumpled the letter, then smoothed it out again. He couldn't afford to be dramatic — the paper was too expensive to waste.

"We need help," he said finally. "Real help. The kind that comes with actual experience and maybe a few connections that don't involve drunk sword masters and gambling addicts."

"Should I fetch Senior Sister Mei?"

"Mei?" Ren blinked. "She's in the kitchen. Probably trying to figure out how to cultivate through soup again."

"No, I mean—" Li Chen pointed toward the back of the hall, where a rickety staircase led to the upper floor. "She's been up there for two hours. Said she was 'meditating on the nature of profit.'"

Ren climbed the stairs, each step creaking like a dying spirit beast. The upper floor was even worse than the main hall — three rooms, two of which had floors, and one that Mei had claimed as her "strategic planning chamber."

She was sitting cross-legged in the center of the room, surrounded by papers. Actual papers. Neatly stacked, organized by color, each one covered in precise handwriting.

"Mei?" Ren stepped inside. "What is all this?"

She looked up, and for a moment, Ren saw something he'd never seen in his first disciple's eyes before: focus. Pure, crystalline focus.

"I've been thinking," she said. "About our business model."

"Our business model is 'don't die' and 'maybe find a way to make money.'"

"No." Mei stood, brushing dust off her robes. "Our business model is *disruption*."

She grabbed a paper covered in diagrams and numbers. Ren recognized her handwriting — messy, chaotic, but somehow always arriving at the right answer.

"The old sects operate on a subscription model," she explained. "Pay for admission, pay for techniques, pay for protection, pay for advancement. Everything costs spirit stones. The bigger the sect, the more they charge, and the more they gatekeep."

"That's... how cultivation works."

"Yes. But it's inefficient." Mei's eyes gleamed. "Think about it. Most cultivators spend decades farming spirit stones just to afford the next breakthrough. They waste time on menial tasks when they could be cultivating. They're stuck in a system that extracts value instead of creating it."

Ren felt a chill run down his spine. "What are you proposing?"

"A marketplace. A platform. Instead of hoarding techniques and resources behind sect walls, we make them accessible. We charge a small fee for access, a percentage for transactions. We become the middleman — the *efficient* middleman."

"That's..."

"Insane? Yes." Mei grinned. "Also potentially revolutionary. If we can create a network where cultivators trade techniques, pills, and services freely — with New Dao Sect taking a small cut — we bypass the old power structures entirely. We become indispensable before they even realize what we're doing."

Ren stared at her. Then at the papers. Then back at her.

"Where did you learn this?"

Mei's grin faltered. "My family. Before... before I left."

She didn't elaborate, and Ren didn't push. Everyone in New Dao Sect had a past they didn't talk about. That was practically a hiring requirement.

"This is brilliant," he said slowly. "But it's also *dangerous*. The old sects won't just ignore us — they'll crush us. We're talking about the Celestial Sword Sect, the Crimson Lotus Palace, the entire established order."

"The established order is fragile," Mei said. "It's built on scarcity and control. If we offer abundance and freedom..."

"We become a target."

"Or we become the future."

Ren looked at the papers again. The numbers made sense. The logic was sound. The risk was astronomical.

"We need that investor meeting," he said finally. "But we don't go in empty-handed. We need something to show them. A proof of concept."

"What kind of proof?"

Ren thought fast. "Our first product. Something small, something we can actually deliver. A technique, a pill formula, a formation — something that proves New Dao Sect can create value."

"We have the chef's recipes," Li Chen said from the doorway. He'd followed them up without Ren noticing. "Senior Sister Fang's cooking. People already say it helps with qi circulation."

"Fang's cooking?" Ren blinked. "She's a chef. An accident cultivator. Her food is good, but it's not a technique."

"It's a *service*," Mei corrected. "And services have value. If we can prove that our methods produce consistent, measurable results — even from unconventional sources — that's proof of concept."

Ren paced the small room. Three disciples, seventeen spirit stones, and a business plan that could get them all killed. It was insane. It was impossible.

It felt right.

"Alright," he said. "Here's the plan. Mei, you're in charge of the proof of concept. I want a presentation ready in three days — something that shows Azure Pond exactly what we're building. Li Chen, you're on logistics. Find out everything you can about the Azure Pavilion, the Jade District, and Consortium Master Huo. I want to know his weaknesses, his preferences, and his deepest fears."

"And you, boss?" Li Chen asked.

Ren smiled, and it wasn't a kind smile. "I'm going to write a business plan so beautiful it makes the Celestial Sword Sect's treasury look like a beggar's bowl."

---

Three days passed like a blade being sharpened.

Ren barely slept. He wrote, rewrote, and burned more paper than he could afford. Mei worked on her presentation, muttering about "disruptive innovation" and "network effects" until even the drunk sword master started avoiding her. Li Chen gathered intelligence the only way he knew how — by drinking with information brokers and gambling with city guards.

By the morning of the meeting, Ren had a plan. A terrible, beautiful, probably-fatal plan.

They left the temple at dawn, the three of them walking through the misty streets of the mortal city below. The Azure Pavilion was in the Jade District, the richest part of the city, where spirit stones flowed like water and cultivators walked with the arrogance of gods.

The pavilion itself was a masterpiece of architectural cultivation. Jade walls that shimmered with embedded formations. A roof of golden tiles that redirected spiritual energy downward. Guards in matching armor who looked like they could kill a Qi Condensation cultivator with their pinky fingers.

Ren straightened his robes — his best set, which meant "the one with the fewest patches" — and walked through the entrance.

The interior was even more opulent. Silk carpets, crystal chandeliers, and a reception desk made from the wood of a thousand-year-old spirit tree. A woman in elegant blue robes sat behind it, her smile professional and empty.

"Ren Wei, New Dao Sect," he said, his voice steady despite his hammering heart. "I have a meeting with Consortium Master Huo."

The woman checked a jade tablet, nodded, and gestured toward a door on the left. "The consortium master will see you now. Your... associates may wait here."

Mei squeezed his arm. "Don't mess this up."

"Thanks for the vote of confidence."

"I mean it. If you mess this up, I'm going to use your corpse as fertilizer for my next business plan."

Ren walked through the door.

The room beyond was a study, lined with bookshelves that held actual tomes — not just cultivation manuals, but histories, philosophies, economics. A man sat behind a desk of polished obsidian, his face unreadable, his robes simple but clearly expensive.

Consortium Master Huo was not what Ren expected. He looked like a retired scholar, not a financial predator. Soft hands, gentle eyes, a faint smile that could mean anything.

"Ren Wei," Huo said, his voice smooth as silk. "Please, sit."

Ren sat. The chair was comfortable. That worried him.

"I've read your proposal," Huo continued. "Fascinating. A 'startup sect,' you call it. Operating outside traditional hierarchies. Using 'agile methodology' and 'minimum viable products.' Tell me, where did you learn these terms?"

"Books," Ren said honestly. "And experience. I worked for the Celestial Sword Sect for five years. I saw how they operate — the waste, the inefficiency, the way they crush innovation because it threatens their power. I thought there had to be a better way."

"And you believe you've found it?"

Ren met his eyes. "I believe I've found *a* way. Whether it's better depends on execution. That's why I'm here. New Dao Sect needs resources to scale. Azure Pond needs new investment opportunities. I think we can help each other."
